name: CyberArk Machine Identity Security (Venafi + Conjur)
description: CyberArk's identity security platform now spans both ends of the machine identity problem: secrets management through Conjur, and certificate and PKI lifecycle through its 2024 acquisition of Venafi. The combined portfolio aims to give organizations a single console for discovering, issuing, rotating, and revoking both credentials and certificates across cloud, on-prem, and CI/CD environments. Through 2025, CyberArk has been integrating Venafi's discovery and context engine directly into its broader Machine Identity Security suite, with new discovery and context capabilities announced that October.
best_fit: Large enterprises that already run CyberArk for privileged access management and want to extend that same governance model to certificates, secrets, and machine credentials under one vendor relationship.
strengths: Combines secrets management (Conjur) and certificate lifecycle (Venafi) under one platform
Deep PAM heritage extends naturally to machine identity governance
Broad enterprise integration ecosystem
watch: CyberArk completed its roughly $1.5B acquisition of Venafi in late 2024 and spent 2025 integrating Venafi's discovery and context engine into its Machine Identity Security portfolio. Buyers evaluating either product on its own should confirm how far that integration has progressed for their specific use case before assuming full feature parity.

name: HashiCorp Vault (IBM)
description: Vault remains the most widely deployed secrets management platform, providing dynamic secrets, encryption-as-a-service, and identity-based access policies across static and ephemeral credentials. Following IBM's $6.4B acquisition of HashiCorp, completed in February 2025, Vault is being positioned alongside Red Hat OpenShift and Ansible as part of IBM's hybrid cloud automation stack. HCP Vault Secrets, the SaaS-only product line launched in 2023, was discontinued in mid-2025, narrowing the product line to Vault Enterprise, Vault Community Edition, and HCP Vault Dedicated.
best_fit: Organizations with existing Terraform or OpenShift investments, or those needing dynamic secrets and fine-grained policy control across a self-managed or hybrid infrastructure footprint.
strengths: Dynamic secrets generation for databases, cloud IAM, and PKI
Largest deployment footprint and ecosystem of any secrets manager
Deep integration with Terraform and the broader HashiCorp/Red Hat stack under IBM
watch: IBM completed its acquisition of HashiCorp in February 2025 and discontinued the standalone HCP Vault Secrets SaaS product, with end-of-life set for mid-2026 and customers directed toward Vault Enterprise, Community Edition, or HCP Vault Dedicated. Organizations on the discontinued tier should plan migration ahead of that cutoff and watch for further consolidation as IBM folds Vault into its Red Hat-centric roadmap.

name: Akeyless
description: Akeyless is a SaaS-delivered secrets management platform built around its Distributed Fragments Cryptography model, which splits encryption keys across multiple cloud regions so no single provider holds a complete key. It covers secrets storage, dynamic secrets, certificate issuance, and increasingly identity for AI agents and machine workloads, positioned as a lighter-weight, cloud-native alternative to operating self-hosted vault infrastructure.
best_fit: Mid-market and cloud-first organizations that want vault-equivalent secrets management without operating self-hosted infrastructure, particularly those already standardized on multi-cloud or CI/CD-heavy environments.
strengths: SaaS-delivered with no infrastructure to operate
Distributed key fragmentation removes single-vendor key custody
Expanding coverage into AI agent and machine identity use cases

name: Astrix Security
description: Astrix is the most established dedicated NHI security platform, built around discovering and governing OAuth tokens, API keys, service accounts, and third-party app-to-app connections across SaaS estates like Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, Salesforce, and GitHub. Its risk-scoring model correlates NHI privileges, third-party app reputation, and observed activity to prioritize which integrations actually need remediation. Since 2024, Astrix has been extending from its SaaS-to-SaaS origin into cloud and on-prem service account coverage, though that expansion is newer than its core SaaS strength.
best_fit: Organizations whose primary NHI exposure is OAuth grants and API keys connecting SaaS applications to each other, particularly enterprises with large Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, or Salesforce footprints and significant third-party app sprawl.
strengths: Category-leading SaaS-to-SaaS NHI discovery and OAuth governance
Risk-based prioritization across third-party app integrations
Broadest SaaS platform coverage among NHI specialists
watch: Astrix's cloud and on-prem NHI coverage is newer and less mature than its SaaS-to-SaaS core, and competitors that started with broader cloud and on-prem scope, such as Oasis Security and Token Security, have been closing the gap on SaaS coverage. Buyers whose primary exposure is cloud workload identity rather than SaaS OAuth sprawl should weigh this carefully.

name: Oasis Security
description: Oasis Security focuses on full-lifecycle governance of non-human identities: discovery, ownership assignment, risk scoring, and automated provisioning through decommissioning across cloud, SaaS, on-prem, and AI agent environments. Its core value proposition is answering who approved an identity and why it still exists, aimed at organizations standing up a formal NHI operating model rather than performing point-in-time discovery. The company raised a $120M Series B in early 2026 to expand both platform coverage and go-to-market.
best_fit: Security and identity teams building a dedicated NHI governance program from scratch, who need lifecycle automation (provisioning, ownership, rotation, and decommissioning) rather than discovery alone.
strengths: Full lifecycle automation from provisioning through decommissioning
Strong ownership and accountability model for service accounts and AI agents
Broad cloud, SaaS, on-prem, and vault integration coverage

name: Token Security
description: Token Security takes an AI-agent-first, intent-driven approach to NHI security, modeling what each machine identity and AI agent was created to do and then flagging privilege and access that exceeds that intent. It emphasizes reducing privilege sprawl and assigning clear ownership over formal governance workflows, positioning itself for organizations where AI agent proliferation and secrets exposure are the most pressing operational risks.
best_fit: Organizations where AI agent sprawl and over-privileged service identities are the dominant concern, and where the priority is rapid privilege reduction and ownership assignment over building a full governance program.
strengths: Intent-based modeling flags privilege drift for AI agents and service identities
Strong focus on operational risk reduction over compliance paperwork
Fast time-to-value for privilege right-sizing

name: Entro Security
description: Entro combines secret discovery and machine identity lifecycle management to reduce secret sprawl and unmanaged credentials across code repositories, CI/CD pipelines, vaults, and cloud environments. The platform has expanded its positioning to cover governance of AI agents and the actions they take, reflecting the broader market shift toward treating agentic AI identities as a first-class NHI category.
best_fit: Organizations whose NHI risk is concentrated in leaked or sprawling secrets across source control and CI/CD, particularly those wanting secret discovery and machine identity governance from a single platform.
strengths: Combines secret discovery with machine identity lifecycle management
Strong CI/CD and source control coverage
Extending governance model to cover AI agent actions

name: Clutch Security
description: Clutch Security is built around zero-trust enforcement and ephemeral, short-lived credentials rather than traditional secrets rotation. Its platform emphasizes continuous validation and detection-and-response for non-human identities, treating compromised or anomalous machine credentials as an incident-response problem rather than purely a governance one. Founded in Tel Aviv in 2023, Clutch raised a $20M Series A in early 2025.
best_fit: Security teams that already have a detection and response function and want NHI security to plug into that workflow, focused on continuous validation and rapid containment of compromised machine credentials rather than a standalone governance program.
strengths: Zero-trust, ephemeral-credential architecture reduces standing secrets exposure
Detection-and-response orientation fits existing SOC workflows
Less reliant on traditional vault-based secrets rotation

name: Aembit
description: Aembit's Workload IAM platform replaces stored credentials with cryptographic workload attestation and just-in-time, policy-driven access, issuing short-lived tokens at the moment of access rather than retrieving long-lived secrets from a vault. Its policy engine evaluates workload identity, environment, and security posture, including integrations with tools like CrowdStrike and Wiz, before brokering credentials. In 2025 Aembit extended coverage to the Microsoft ecosystem (Azure, Active Directory, Entra ID) and introduced an MCP Identity Gateway for brokering AI agent access to MCP servers.
best_fit: Engineering and platform teams adopting a secretless architecture who want to eliminate stored credentials for service-to-service and AI agent access, rather than just rotating them faster.
strengths: Secretless architecture removes stored credentials that can leak or need rotation
Policy engine incorporates security posture signals from tools like CrowdStrike and Wiz
MCP Identity Gateway addresses AI agent-to-tool access specifically

name: SPIFFE / SPIRE
description: SPIFFE (Secure Production Identity Framework for Everyone) and its reference implementation SPIRE are CNCF graduated projects that define an open standard for issuing cryptographic identities, called SVIDs, to workloads independent of network location or cloud provider. SPIRE handles attestation and identity issuance; it does not provide policy management, discovery, or governance on its own, which is why most commercial workload identity and NHI governance platforms either build on SPIFFE or interoperate with it. The IETF's WIMSE working group is extending similar concepts toward broader interoperability with OAuth-based machine-to-machine standards.
best_fit: Platform engineering teams building their own workload identity layer on open standards, or evaluating commercial NHI platforms and wanting to understand the underlying identity primitives those platforms build on.
strengths: Open standard with CNCF graduated status and broad ecosystem support
Cloud- and platform-neutral cryptographic workload identity via SVIDs
Forms the identity substrate for many commercial workload identity products
watch: SPIFFE/SPIRE provides identity issuance and attestation but not governance, discovery, or lifecycle management, so organizations adopting it directly should expect to build or buy additional tooling for those layers. Watch the IETF WIMSE effort, which aims to bridge SPIFFE-style workload identity with OAuth and OIDC machine-to-machine flows.

name: Teleport
description: Teleport provides identity-based, secretless access to infrastructure, including servers, Kubernetes clusters, databases, and internal web applications, built on short-lived certificates rather than standing credentials or shared secrets. Its Machine ID product extends the same certificate-based identity model to CI/CD pipelines, bots, and service-to-service workloads, replacing long-lived API keys and SSH keys with SPIFFE-compatible workload identities.
best_fit: Engineering teams that want to eliminate SSH keys, database passwords, and long-lived CI/CD tokens by replacing infrastructure and workload access with a single certificate-based identity proxy.
strengths: Unifies human infrastructure access and machine/CI-CD identity under one certificate-based model
Short-lived certificates eliminate standing SSH keys and static tokens
SPIFFE-compatible Machine ID for service-to-service workloads

name: Microsoft Entra Workload ID
description: Microsoft Entra Workload ID extends Microsoft's identity platform to applications, services, and automation tools, issuing managed identities and federated credentials so workloads can authenticate to Azure resources and Microsoft 365 without stored secrets. For organizations already standardized on Entra ID for human identity, it provides a natural extension to non-human identities, though its governance and discovery capabilities are narrower than dedicated NHI platforms and strongest within the Azure and Microsoft 365 estate.
best_fit: Organizations heavily standardized on Azure and Microsoft 365 that want workload identity tightly integrated with their existing Entra ID tenant, rather than a separate cross-cloud NHI layer.
strengths: Native integration with Azure resources and Microsoft 365 via managed identities
Federated credentials eliminate stored secrets for GitHub Actions and Kubernetes workloads calling Azure
Single identity plane with existing Entra ID human identity governance

name: AWS IAM & Secrets Manager
description: AWS IAM roles and IAM Identity Center provide the native non-human identity primitives for AWS workloads, including instance roles, federated OIDC identities for CI/CD and Kubernetes service accounts, and cross-account role assumption, while AWS Secrets Manager handles storage and rotation of credentials that can't be replaced with IAM roles. Together they cover the majority of NHI use cases for organizations operating primarily within AWS, but provide limited visibility into multi-cloud, SaaS, or on-prem service accounts without additional tooling.
best_fit: AWS-centric organizations whose NHI footprint is concentrated within a single cloud and who want to maximize use of IAM roles before reaching for third-party secrets or NHI governance tooling.
strengths: IAM roles eliminate stored credentials for most AWS-to-AWS workload access
OIDC federation for GitHub Actions and Kubernetes removes long-lived access keys
Deep, no-additional-cost integration across the AWS service catalog
watch: An estimated 200,000 to 500,000 organizations still rely on AWS Secrets Manager to store credentials that could be replaced with IAM role-based federation. As third-party workload identity and NHI governance platforms increasingly integrate directly with Secrets Manager, expect continued migration pressure toward role-based, secretless patterns even within single-cloud AWS environments.

name: Google Cloud Workload Identity Federation
description: Google Cloud's Workload Identity Federation lets external workloads, such as GitHub Actions runners, on-prem servers, or other clouds' compute, exchange their own identity tokens for short-lived Google Cloud credentials without storing service account keys. Combined with GKE Workload Identity for Kubernetes pods, it provides a secretless path for the majority of GCP-bound workload access, with Secret Manager handling remaining credential storage needs.
best_fit: GCP-centric organizations, particularly those running GKE, that want to eliminate downloaded service account key files in favor of federated, short-lived credentials.
strengths: Workload Identity Federation removes the need for exported service account key files
GKE Workload Identity binds Kubernetes service accounts directly to IAM
Native integration with BigQuery and other GCP-native services

name: Okta
description: Okta's Workforce Identity and Customer Identity platforms have extended into machine-to-machine and AI agent identity, framing the company's positioning around securing AI, machine, and human identity under one policy and lifecycle model. Its core strength remains workforce SSO, lifecycle management, and adaptive access policy across thousands of SaaS application integrations, with machine identity capabilities layered on through OAuth client credential flows and the Auth0 platform for developer-facing use cases.
best_fit: Organizations already standardized on Okta for workforce identity and SSO that want to extend the same policy framework to API clients, service accounts, and AI agents without introducing a separate NHI platform.
strengths: Largest catalog of pre-built SaaS application integrations
Unified policy model spanning workforce, customer, and increasingly machine identity
Auth0 platform covers developer-facing machine-to-machine OAuth flows

name: SailPoint
description: SailPoint's identity security platform, built around its identity governance and administration heritage, has extended its certification, access review, and policy enforcement model to cover non-human identities, including service accounts, bots, and increasingly AI agents, alongside human workforce identities. Its strength is bringing NHIs into the same compliance and access certification workflows that enterprises already run for employee access.
best_fit: Enterprises with mature identity governance programs that want non-human identities folded into existing access certification, compliance reporting, and audit workflows rather than managed through a separate NHI tool.
strengths: Extends established access certification and compliance workflows to non-human identities
AI-assisted policy recommendations across large identity populations
Deep enterprise identity governance heritage and audit reporting

name: GitGuardian
description: GitGuardian started as a secrets detection platform, scanning git history, pull requests, and public repositories for leaked credentials, and has expanded into a broader NHI security platform covering secrets inventory, non-human identity discovery, and remediation workflows tied directly into developer pipelines. Its 2026 research reported roughly 29 million secrets detected on public GitHub in 2025, a year-over-year increase that underpins much of the market's urgency around NHI.
best_fit: Engineering and application security teams whose primary NHI exposure is leaked secrets in source code and CI/CD pipelines, who want detection and remediation embedded directly in developer workflows.
strengths: Industry-leading secrets detection across git history and public repositories
Remediation workflows integrate directly into pull requests and CI/CD
Expanding from secrets detection into broader NHI inventory and governance

name: Wiz
description: Wiz's cloud security platform includes a cloud infrastructure entitlement management module that maps the effective permissions of human and non-human identities across multi-cloud environments, correlating those permissions with vulnerability and exposure data from the rest of the platform. Its NHI-relevant value is contextual: identifying which over-privileged service accounts and roles are also reachable from an internet-facing vulnerability, rather than standalone identity governance.
best_fit: Cloud security teams that already use Wiz for vulnerability and exposure management and want entitlement and identity risk correlated with that same exposure graph, rather than managed as a separate tool.
strengths: Correlates identity entitlements with vulnerability and exposure data in a single graph
Agentless multi-cloud deployment consistent with the rest of the Wiz platform
Strong visibility into toxic combinations of over-privilege and exposure
watch: Google announced an agreement to acquire Wiz in March 2025 for approximately $32 billion, and the deal continued moving through regulatory review into 2026. Organizations evaluating Wiz for long-term NHI and CIEM strategy should track how the acquisition affects multi-cloud neutrality, particularly Wiz's AWS and Azure coverage, once it closes.

name: Sonrai Security
description: Sonrai Security is a cloud infrastructure entitlement management platform focused on mapping and continuously monitoring the relationships between identities, both human and non-human, data, and the permissions connecting them across multi-cloud environments. Its graph-based model is built specifically to surface identity and data access risk, with less emphasis on the broader vulnerability and posture management that platforms like Wiz bundle alongside entitlement management.
best_fit: Organizations whose primary multi-cloud risk concern is identity-to-data access paths, meaning who can reach what sensitive data and through which chain of role assumptions, rather than broader vulnerability management.
strengths: Graph-based identity-to-data access path analysis across multi-cloud
Purpose-built entitlement management without the overhead of a full cloud security platform
Continuous monitoring of effective permissions, not just assigned permissions

name: Fortanix
description: Fortanix combines hardware security module-backed key management, secrets management, and certificate lifecycle capabilities within confidential computing environments, aimed at organizations with strict cryptographic key custody and data-in-use protection requirements. Its data security platform is positioned as infrastructure for regulated industries needing both machine identity and encryption key management under a unified, FIPS-validated foundation.
best_fit: Regulated enterprises in financial services, healthcare, and government that need HSM-backed key management and certificate lifecycle management combined with confidential computing guarantees for data-in-use.
strengths: HSM-backed, FIPS-validated key management and certificate issuance
Confidential computing integration for data-in-use protection
Unified platform for encryption keys, secrets, and certificates
